cataloging_subject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Corpus MS 198/Furnivall representation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and twenty-four-tale collection as transmitted principally in Corpus Christi College, Oxford, MS 198 and represented by Frederick J. Furnivall's one-manuscript Chaucer Society edition. This cataloging unit preserves the witness-specific order A, B-alpha, F, D, E, F, G, C, B-beta, H, I; the first F occurrence contains the Squire and the second the Franklin. It includes explicit supplied passages, the non-counted Tale of Gamelyn, supplementary links, and visible edition apparatus without conflating the abstract Canterbury Tales, physical manuscripts, printed edition, digital facsimiles, U-M encoding, or repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: The Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; anonymous responsibility for the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn
catalog_summary.date: Composed chiefly in the late 1380s and 1390s; Corpus MS 198 copied 1401-1450; source edition issued 1868-1884
catalog_summary.region: England; manuscript produced in London; work associated with late-medieval London literary language

notes: The Corpus MS 198/Furnivall qualifier defines this cataloging representation; it is not part of the conventional work title.

titles.value: The Corpus MS (Corpus Christi Coll., Oxford) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

agents.name: Anonymous author of The Tale of Gamelyn
notes: A responsibility placeholder for the non-Chaucerian tale, not a claim that its historical author was a collective.

scope.description: The eleven sequence containers representing the ten conventional Canterbury Tales fragments; excludes Gamelyn and edition apparatus

notes: This does not assign authorship of the supplementary links.

scope.description: The 1868-1884 one-manuscript edition and its apparatus, not medieval authorship
Transcription of Corpus MS 198 with explicitly marked supplies from other manuscripts

date_statements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400

scope.description: The Chaucerian core, not Gamelyn or modern apparatus

notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame.

scope.description: The Corpus-MS readings within current fols. 2r-266v, beginning at General Prologue A73 and ending after Parson I290; every passage supplied from another witness is excluded

notes: A witness-production range, not a composition date.

date_statements.display: Chaucer Society edition issued in seven parts, 1868-1884; combined title page dated 1868-1879

scope.description: The exact edition-defined representation and apparatus

places.label: Southwark
notes: Narrative departure point, not asserted as the composition site.

scope.description: The Corpus-MS readings within current fols. 2r-266v, excluding all Furnivall supplies; London is the production place cataloged for the physical witness
Literary-language association, not a secure composition site
Departure point in the pilgrimage frame

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin rubrics and quotations and the incidental Middle French source phrase cited in Melibee's apparatus do not become work-level language facets.

form_statements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; the Retraction is prose epilogue and is not counted as a third prose tale.

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Pilgrims traveling from Southwark toward Thomas Becket's shrine at Canterbury agree to tell stories. Chaucer's unfinished frame juxtaposes social portraits, literary genres, moral argument, and competing performances. This representation preserves the distinctive Corpus MS 198/Furnivall order, its lacunae and supplements, the non-counted Gamelyn, and edition-level apparatus.

The semantic inventory has sixty-nine parts: eleven witness-order containers representing ten conventional fragments; forty-eight encoded components within them, including exactly twenty-four counted tales and the non-counted Tale of Gamelyn; the Christ Church Nun's Priest end-link; a five-text appendix with its container; and a visual-apparatus container with two suites. Group F recurs in two non-contiguous containers.

The primary Corpus witness begins at General Prologue A73 and ends imperfectly in the Parson's Tale after I290. Furnivall fills identified passages from Arch. Selden B.14 and several other manuscripts; these supplies are not attributed to Corpus MS 198. Physical, editorial, encoding, facsimile, and repository layers are modeled separately in lineage.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales: twenty-two verse and two prose. The 616 LG and 17,403 L counts are semantic XML element counts after excluding the single 902-line Gamelyn LG; they include verse links and supplied passages and are not a critical lineation. Prologues, links, Retraction, apparatus, and Gamelyn do not increment the tale count.

content_parts.label: Group A, Fragment I
content_parts.summary: General Prologue, Knight, Miller, Reeve, Cook, and the witness's appended Gamelyn.

content_parts.label: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: Pilgrim portraits and agreement to the tale-telling game.
notes: Lines A1-72 and further gaps are supplied from Bodleian MS Arch. Selden B.14; Corpus MS 198 begins at A73.

content_parts.label: The Knight's Tale
content_parts.summary: Romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

content_parts.label: Miller's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Miller interrupts the planned order and introduces his fabliau.

content_parts.label: The Miller's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alison, John, and Absolon.

content_parts.label: Reeve's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller.

content_parts.label: The Reeve's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of the miller Symkyn and two clerks.

content_parts.label: Cook's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Cook responds to the Reeve.

content_parts.label: The Cook's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fragmentary tale of Perkyn Revelour.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Gamelyn
content_parts.summary: Non-Chaucerian tale copied in Corpus MS 198 and printed by Furnivall as an appendix to Group A.
notes: Present in the primary witness but excluded from the conventional count of twenty-four Chaucerian tales.

content_parts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
content_parts.summary: Man of Law sequence, followed in the manuscript by an instruction that the Squire comes next.

content_parts.label: Man of Law's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls upon the Man of Law.

content_parts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prologue and three-part tale of Constance.

content_parts.label: Man of Law's End-link
content_parts.summary: Witness-specific terminal link directing that the Squire follows.

content_parts.label: Group F, Squire Segment (Fragment VII)
content_parts.summary: The Squire's Tale, separated in this representation from the later Franklin segment.
notes: One conventional Fragment VII is split into two non-contiguous sequence containers by the Corpus/Furnivall order.

content_parts.label: The Squire's Tale
content_parts.summary: Unfinished romance of Cambyuskan's gifts, represented in two parts.

content_parts.label: Group D, Fragment V
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

content_parts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble
content_parts.summary: Autobiographical argument about marriage and authority.
notes: Several lacunae are supplied from Arch. Selden B.14 and explicitly bracketed.

content_parts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
content_parts.summary: Arthurian romance of sovereignty in marriage.

content_parts.label: Wife-Friar Link
content_parts.summary: Friar and Summoner exchange before the Friar's tale.

content_parts.label: The Friar's Tale
content_parts.summary: Satire of a corrupt summoner and a devil.

content_parts.label: Friar-Summoner Link
content_parts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar.

content_parts.label: The Summoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Satire of a friar and the distribution of a fart.

content_parts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
content_parts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence; the Corpus Merchant text ends at E2318.

content_parts.label: Clerk's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls upon the Clerk.

content_parts.label: The Clerk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Six-part tale of Griselda with envoy.

content_parts.label: The Merchant's Tale
content_parts.summary: Tale of January, May, and Damian through E2318 in Corpus MS 198.
notes: Furnivall prints E2319-2418 separately from Arch. Selden B.14 in the appendix.

content_parts.label: Group F, Franklin Segment (Fragment VII)
content_parts.summary: The Franklin's Tale, returning to Group F after the D and E sequences.
notes: This is the second of two non-contiguous containers representing conventional Fragment VII.

content_parts.label: The Franklin's Tale
content_parts.summary: Breton lay of Dorigen, Arveragus, and Aurelius.

content_parts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
content_parts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

content_parts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prologue and saint's life of Cecilia.

content_parts.label: Second Nun-Canon Link
content_parts.summary: Arrival of the Canon and his Yeoman.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble
content_parts.summary: The Canon departs and the Yeoman introduces alchemy.

content_parts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Alchemy tale exposing fraudulent practice.

content_parts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
content_parts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

content_parts.label: The Physician's Tale
content_parts.summary: Tragedy of Virginia and Apius.

content_parts.label: Physician-Pardoner Link
content_parts.summary: The Host reacts to Virginia and calls upon the Pardoner.

content_parts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his preaching methods.

content_parts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters seeking Death.

content_parts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
content_parts.summary: Shipman, Prioress, Chaucer, Melibee, Monk, and Nun's Priest sequence.

content_parts.label: The Shipman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, his wife, and a monk.

content_parts.label: Shipman-Prioress Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls upon the Prioress.

content_parts.label: The Prioress's Tale
content_parts.summary: Marian miracle narrative.

content_parts.label: Prioress-Thopas Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls upon Chaucer the pilgrim.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
content_parts.summary: Parodic tail-rhyme romance interrupted by the Host.

content_parts.label: Thopas-Melibee Link
content_parts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and requests a prose tale.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Melibee
content_parts.summary: Prose counsel narrative of Melibee and Prudence.
notes: A large omitted passage is supplied from Laud MS 600; a French phrase at paragraph 2765 cites Royal MS 19 C VII as a source-language analogue rather than an English base text.

content_parts.label: Melibee-Monk Link
content_parts.summary: The Host responds to Melibee and calls upon the Monk.

content_parts.label: The Monk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Series of tragedies of fallen great figures.

content_parts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link
content_parts.summary: The Knight stops the Monk and the Host calls upon the Nun's Priest.

content_parts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
content_parts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer, Pertelote, and the fox.

content_parts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
content_parts.summary: Manciple head-link and tale.

content_parts.label: Manciple's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Host's exchange with the Cook and call for the Manciple.

content_parts.label: The Manciple's Tale
content_parts.summary: Moral tale of Phoebus and the crow.

content_parts.label: Group I, Fragment X
content_parts.summary: Parson head-link, editorial analysis, prose tale, and Retraction.

content_parts.label: Blank-Parson Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls for the final tale and the Parson promises moral prose.

content_parts.label: Contents and Analysis of the Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's analytical contents preceding the tale.

content_parts.label: The Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prose penitential treatise; Corpus MS 198 ends imperfectly after I290 and Furnivall continues chiefly from Arch. Selden B.14.

content_parts.label: Retraction
content_parts.summary: Closing prose retraction; the last portion of I1086 through I1092 is supplied from Hatton donat.1.
notes: A distinct epilogue, not a third prose tale.

content_parts.label: Nun's Priest-Manciple End-link
content_parts.summary: Sixteen-line link transcribed from Christ Church MS 152, ff. 173v-174r.

content_parts.label: Furnivall Textual Appendix
content_parts.summary: Five supplied continuations and links printed on pp. 1*-8*.

content_parts.label: End of the Merchant's Tale
content_parts.summary: Merchant E2319-2418 from Arch. Selden B.14.

content_parts.label: Merchant-Squire Link
content_parts.summary: Link after the Merchant's Tale from Arch. Selden B.14.

content_parts.label: Clerk-Merchant Link
content_parts.summary: Merchant's introductory link after the Clerk from Arch. Selden B.14.

content_parts.label: Squire's Head-link
content_parts.summary: Eight-line Squire introduction from Arch. Selden B.14.

content_parts.label: Squire-Franklin Link
content_parts.summary: Link from Laud MS 600, with F679-680 separately attributed to an unresolved 'Haistwell MS'.
notes: The historical Haistwell shelfmark has not been resolved and is not silently conflated with another manuscript.

content_parts.label: Edition Visual Apparatus
content_parts.summary: Plate and woodcut suites announced for the Furnivall edition.
notes: The audited Internet Archive carrier lacks nine of the twenty-three indicated plates and the nine-plate suite of six tellers and allegorical figures, which the edition note says may never have been issued.

content_parts.label: Ellesmere-derived Teller Plates in the Corpus-MS Edition
content_parts.summary: Twenty-three teller plates indicated in the contents; fourteen survive in the cached carrier.

content_parts.label: Cambridge Gg.4.27 Teller and Allegorical Drawings
content_parts.summary: Six teller drawings and six allegorical figures announced as a nine-plate suite.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML structure and semantic verse audit for agz8235.
evidence.locator: 15 DIV1, 54 DIV2, 67 DIV3, 617 LG, 18,305 L, 756 PB; Gamelyn 1 LG/902 L
evidence.summary: Confirms a contiguous 756-page-image sequence, exact XML nesting, 24 conventional tales, and 616 LG/17,403 L after exclusion of the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn.

evidence.summary: Current public item route, title, creator attribution, table of contents, and U-M rights designation.

evidence.citation: Companion lineage record for Furnivall's Corpus-MS edition, its physical carrier, witness sources, issue history, and audited apparatus.
evidence.locator: 740-page PDF; main pp. 1-700 [i.e. 705], p. 698 Christ Church link, appendix pp. 1*-8*, contents and plate inventory
evidence.summary: Fixes the exact witness-specific order, printed page spans, supplied texts, issue history, and surviving versus announced visual apparatus.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Corpus Christi College MS 198, Canterbury Tales.
evidence.summary: Official metadata for shelfmark, 1401-1450 date, London origin, Middle English, extent, Scribe D, imperfect endpoints, decoration, provenance, and image-use terms.

evidence.citation: Harvard University, Geoffrey Chaucer Website, General Prologue.
evidence.summary: Supports the pilgrimage frame, Southwark departure, Canterbury destination, and estates portraits.

evidence.citation: Harvard University, Geoffrey Chaucer Website, Fabliaux.

evidence.citation: Harvard University, Geoffrey Chaucer Website, The Prioress's Tale.
Harvard University, Geoffrey Chaucer Website, The Second Nun's Tale.

open_questions.question: What order, if any, did Chaucer intend for the ten fragments?
next_steps: Treat A, B-alpha, F, D, E, F, G, C, B-beta, H, I as the Corpus/Furnivall arrangement, not an authorial fact.
Preserve the two non-contiguous F segments in discovery displays.

open_questions.question: What independent authorship and work status should be assigned to Gamelyn and the supplementary links?
next_steps: Keep Gamelyn and links at item_count 0 in the conventional tale count.
Create independent component records only after text-specific research.

open_questions.question: Which present manuscript or former shelfmark is Furnivall's 'Haistwell MS' source for Squire-Franklin link F679-680?
next_steps: Preserve the historical label as unresolved.
Do not conflate it with Hatton donat.1 or another named witness without catalog evidence.

open_questions.question: Should the twenty-four conventional tales receive independent work records?
next_steps: Retain the complete nested inventory here.
Avoid inheriting whole-collection claims into future tale-level records.

notes: The twenty-four-tale count is semantic: prologues, links, interruptions, Retraction, apparatus, and the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn do not increment it.
Corpus MS 198 begins at A73 and ends imperfectly after Parson I290; bracketed supplies are modeled as edition-to-source relations, never as physical contents of the Corpus witness.
Eleven representation-order containers correspond to ten conventional fragments because Group F/Fragment VII is split and recurs.
The Royal MS 19 C VII Middle French phrase is incidental source apparatus and therefore does not add a work-level Middle French language facet.
No reusable modeling problem beyond the project's existing boundary, edition, digital, and rights case lessons was discovered, so no shared skill file is changed on this isolated branch.
